title: Surveillance State
html:
A recurring lens Yahir applies to anything involving identification, tracking, or aggregated data. The first explicit surfacing is the COVID-era 2020 03 30 facebook pandemic watchlist, where 17-year-old Yahir writes in all-caps: "SURVIELLANCE OF POEPLE ORWELLIAN STYLE, because of contact tracing" — pulling China tracking apps and post-pandemic infrastructure into the same frame. The rest of that note's futures list (mass digital migration, India infrastructure, science-prioritization) treats surveillance as one of the structural shifts to watch.

The 2025 OSU biometrics screenshots (2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 21 pm e4e5aad8, 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 38 pm 56e50e24, 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 41 04 pm 5f2b64af) sharpen the concept with technical content. The Jain/Pankanti/Prabhakar survey makes the case that biometrics is a sword that cuts both ways: health-insurance discrimination from retinal scans is the dystopia, biometric-locked credit cards and patient-controlled medical records are the utopia — the deciding factor is legislation + encryption. The negative recognition framing in the airport-watchlist worked example is the most operative version: a system that narrows from millions to hundreds and lets humans decide, and if you're not in the criminal database, the system doesn't remember you. A civil-liberties primitive worth keeping.

The June 2025 mobile-malware screenshots (2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 20 pm 44f00f66, 2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 26 pm 7f8b8bc5) add the economic surveillance layer: 28 of 46 documented mobile malware samples exfiltrate location, contacts, browser history, IMEI — and the 2008 black-market price sheet (bank credentials $10–$1,000, credit cards $0.10–$25, ad libraries earning $1.90–$9.50/user/month) tells you exactly what surveillance is worth. The deepest point: surveillance is rarely run by states alone — the data economy buys most of it.

The lens that ties all three eras together: surveillance is not abstract; it has prices, technical primitives, and policy levers, and Yahir consistently treats it as a design problem rather than a moral panic.

Source summaries

  • 2020 03 30 facebook pandemic watchlist — first explicit framing
  • 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 21 pm e4e5aad8 — biometric modalities
  • 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 38 pm 56e50e24 — FMR/FNMR + negative recognition
  • 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 41 04 pm 5f2b64af — privacy/security tradeoffs
  • 2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 20 pm 44f00f66 — exfiltration economics
  • 2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 26 pm 7f8b8bc5 — credential black market

Connections

  • biometric recognition / biometric template / biometric privacy tradeoff — the technical primitives
  • mobile malware / credential black market pricing — the economic side
  • post pandemic future thinking — the broader frame
  • disruption — adjacent lens (sometimes friction wins)
  • china — formative example of state-scale surveillance
  • two factor authentication bypass — failure mode
